figures.caption: Figure 6. Genome-wide heterozygosity across generations for reciprocal selection 930 trajectories. Mean genome-wide heterozygosity was measured in replicate populations (n = 10 931 per trajectory) evolving under A→C (early-to-late reproduction) and C→A (late-to-early 932 reproduction) selection regimes. Boxplots show the distribution of mean heterozygosity across 933 replicates at each sampled generation, including earlier time points. A→C populations exhibit 934 increasing heterozygosity over time, whereas C→A populations show a progressive decline. Stars 935 above boxes indicate significant differences between founder and midpoint populations based on 936 statistical tests. 937

Figure 7. Deep sequencing reveals allele frequency variation at sites previously classified 939 as fixed. Histograms depict the minor allele frequency (MAF) of SNPs in the A-type1 population 940 sequenced at ~800× coverage. Gray bars represent a random subset of SNPs equal in number 941 to the pseudo-fixed sites identified in standard sequencing data, while purple bars indicate sites 942 classified as pseudo-fixed under standard coverage (Bonferroni-corrected GLM, 𝑝 < 0.005). 943 Pseudo-fixed sites are overwhelmingly skewed toward low-frequency alleles, indicating that these 944 loci appear fixed under standard coverage but retain hidden variation at higher sequencing depth. 945 Permutation testing (10,000 replicates) confirmed that the observed mean MAF of pseudo-fixed 946 sites (0.0063) is dramatically lower than expected under random sampling (mean null = 0.1745, 947 SD = 0.00037; Z = –450.79, 𝑃 < 10−4), demonstrating that these pseudo-fixed sites represent a 948 significant depletion of minor alleles relative to genome-wide expectations. 949

Figure 2. Waves of Convergence selection. Differences in mean age-specific adult mortality 898 plotted as a function of age from egg. (A) C→A trajectory: differences between C→A populations 899 and A-type founder populations after ~33 (magenta) and ~143 (red) generations of early-900 reproduction selection, shown relative to the baseline difference between C- and A-type founders 901 (purple). (B) A→C trajectory: differences between A→C populations and C-type founder 902 populations after ~12 (light blue) and ~51 (dark blue) generations of delayed-reproduction 903 selection, shown relative to the baseline difference between A- and C-type founders (purple). 904 Points indicate mean mortality differences at each age; lines show smoothed trends. 905

Figure 10. Leave-one-out analysis comparing allele frequency changes at target SNPs 970 identified from all but one replicate population to matched control SNPs. This analysis was 971 carried out independently for the A→C and C→A trajectories. For each LOO iteration, the median 972 allele frequency shift of target and matched control SNPs in the held-out replicate is shown, 973 genome-wide and separately for each major chromosome arm for the A→C and C→A trajectories. 974 Target SNPs were defined using FDR < 0.05 and ≥ 0.02 total change in allele frequency in the 975 training set, with controls drawn from non-significant SNPs. Points represent medians from 976 individual LOO iterations; the dashed line indicates no net allele frequency change. 977

Figure 3. Antiparallel genomic trajectories under reciprocal selection. Lines indicate allele-907 frequency trajectories of replicate populations for A→C (blue) and C→A (red) across PC1 and 908 PC2. Facet labels indicate the percentage of variance explained by each PC. Each line 909 represents a replicate population, with points corresponding to successive generations (x-axis 910 labels show generation numbers for each trajectory). 911

Figure 8. PCA of SNP frequencies after reciprocal selection shifts. Principal component 952 analysis of SNP frequencies at the last collected timepoints for populations of the A→C (light blue) 953 and C→A (light red) trajectories, alongside founder A (dark red) and C (dark blue) populations. 954 Ellipses represent 95% confidence intervals around group centroids. C→A populations, which 955 experienced 182 generations of selection, cluster tightly with the A founders, whereas A→C 956 populations, with 65 generations of selection, show greater spread and incomplete convergence 957 with C founders. 958

Figure 4. Genome-wide distribution of SNPs with Bonferroni-significant selection × 913 generation interactions. The Manhattan plot shows –log₁₀-transformed Bonferroni-adjusted p-914 values for the interaction term across all major chromosome arms (2L, 2R, 3L, 3R, X). SNP 915 positions are shown in megabases (Mb) along the x-axis, and chromosomes are colored 916 individually for clarity. The dashed horizontal line indicates the α = 0.05 significance threshold 917 applied to Bonferroni-adjusted p-values. Significant SNPs are broadly distributed across the 918 genome, with no single chromosome dominating the signal, consistent with a highly polygenic 919 response and anti-parallel evolutionary trajectories across selection regimes. 920

Figure 9. Genome-wide differentiation of SNP frequencies after reciprocal selection shifts. 960 Manhattan plots show beta-binomial GLMM results comparing allele frequencies at the final 961 sampled time point in reciprocally selected populations to long-established populations 962 maintained under the same selection regime: (A) A→C populations relative to C-type founders 963 and (B) C→A populations relative to A-type founders. Each point represents a SNP plotted by 964 chromosomal position (Mb) on the x-axis and −log₁₀(Bonferroni-adjusted p-value) on the y-axis. 965 Dashed horizontal lines denote a significance threshold of α = 0.05 on the Bonferroni-adjusted p-966 values. Chromosomes are color-coded as 2L (light blue), 2R (green), 3L (red), 3R (blue), and X 967 (orange). 968

Figure 5. Genome-wide SNP overlap among Bonferroni-significant effects. The UpSet plot 922 displays the number of SNPs with Bonferroni-significant effects for generation, selection, and the 923 selection × generation interaction. Notably, 134,709 SNPs exhibit significant selection × 924 generation interactions, highlighting pervasive, genome-wide divergence in allele frequency 925 trajectories between reciprocal selection regimes. The plot illustrates that while some loci also 926 show main effects of generation or treatment alone, the interaction effect dominates, consistent 927 with highly polygenic, anti-parallel evolutionary responses. 928
